\section{The one action that helps: give up correctly}\label{sec:giveup}

\cref{sec:boundary} drew the line: spending the verdict in-loop is flat-to-harmful, and the one
in-loop action that paid off was the negative one. This section measures it. A byte-clean detector
(\cref{sec:bench}) raises an alarm but must be careful what it does next: most actions cross from
\emph{detecting} into \emph{fixing}, and a fix authors a correction the agent will trust ---
breaking the byte-clean rule the detector was built on. Exactly one action does not cross that line:
\textbf{withhold compute}. Stop a clearly-doomed run and hand its remaining budget to one that
might still win. Stopping authors no correction, so it cannot mislead. Of every action we measured,
this negative one was the only survivor.

\subsection{Error-gated give-up is accuracy-free}\label{sec:giveup-accuracy}

The gate is simple. It watches each tool the agent uses. When a single tool
returns \emph{K} structured errors in a row --- errors written by the
environment, not by the agent --- \textbf{and} its most recent call
is still erroring, the gate proposes a halt. The second condition matters: the
run must be failing \emph{now}, not have failed earlier and recovered.

The question is whether this ever stops a run that would have \emph{won} ---
a \emph{false-abandon}, the gate halting a run that would actually have succeeded.
It is the only costly mistake the gate can make, so it is the number that has to be
zero.

\begin{table}[t]
  \centering
  \caption{\cref{tab:giveup}. Give-up on Toolathlon at the sound threshold \emph{K}=3,
across 22 models. The naive gate halts on a bare repeat; the error-gated gate also requires an
environment-authored error with the latest call still failing. \emph{Fires} counts every run the
gate would halt; \emph{false-abandon} counts only halts landing on one of the 1,634 winners (the
costly mistake). Wilson-95 is the 95\%-confidence upper bound on the true false-abandon rate.}
  \label{tab:giveup}
  \begin{tabular}{lrlrll}
    \toprule
    K & naive repeat: fires & naive: false-abandon & error-gated: fires & error-gated: false-abandon & Wilson-95 upper \\
    \midrule
    \textbf{3} & 30 & \textbf{6} & 0 & \textbf{0} & \textbf{0.0023} \\
    \bottomrule
  \end{tabular}
\end{table}

At \emph{K}$\ge$3 the error-gated gate false-abandons \textbf{zero}
winners --- across all 22 models, frontier to weak. Its Wilson-95 upper bound
is just 0.0023, so even the worst the true rate could plausibly be is near zero.
The same threshold is sound on a second, independent benchmark,
EnterpriseOps-Gym (a ServiceNow operations gym run on gemini-2.5-flash):
\emph{K}$\ge$3 false-abandons zero winners on both arms there, while
\emph{K}=2 is unsound (it halts one winner). Two different benchmarks, one safe
threshold.

The naive gate --- halt on a bare repeat, no error condition --- false-abandons
\textbf{6} winners at \emph{K}=3, every one the same model (grok-4) doing the same harmless
thing: polling ``is it ready yet?'' until the resource became ready and the run
\emph{succeeded}. A repeated answer is not an error. The byte-clean rule saves the gate: the signal is
the environment-authored \emph{error}, not the repeat --- ``repeated tool call~$\rightarrow$
halt'' kills pollers, ``repeated tool \emph{error}~$\rightarrow$ halt'' does not, because
a poller's repeats are not errors and the agent cannot forge the error bytes.

\begin{figure*}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{fig_giveup_cross_benchmark}
  \caption{\cref{fig:giveup}. Winning runs wrongly halted (lower is better). \textbf{Left:} as \emph{K} varies, the error-gated gate (green) reaches zero at \emph{K}$\ge$3 on both benchmarks; the naive ``halt on any repeat'' gate (red) never does, because it keeps killing pollers. \textbf{Right:} at \emph{K}=3 by model --- the error-gated gate halts zero winners on all 22 models; the only non-zero bar is the naive gate on grok-4.}
  \label{fig:giveup}
\end{figure*}

So blunt a gate works because, on the \emph{K} statistic --- how many errors a tool stacks up
in a row --- the winning runs and the doomed runs fall into almost non-overlapping ranges: winners
rarely stack errors, doomed runs do. That a clean class separation collapses to a
threshold test is nothing new; it is the separable regime of any threshold classifier, and the
SPRT/CUSUM tradition (below) is built on exactly such run-length count statistics. The part that
matters here is the statistic's \emph{input}: the count is over \emph{environment-authored error
bytes the agent cannot forge}. That is what makes the integer threshold at \emph{K}$\ge$3 both
sound (zero false-abandon, measured) and non-gameable. You do not need a probabilistic model when
the two populations barely touch and the statistic separating them is one the agent cannot
author.

The saving follows. At \emph{K}=3 the gate fires on $\sim$7\% of runs (the thrashing tail) and on each
suppresses a median $\sim$53\% of its tool calls (the doomed back half) --- pooled across both
EnterpriseOps-Gym arms, \textbf{11.4\%} of total tokens, saved at zero false-abandon. Across five
recorded run-sets it saved 3.5k--43.7k real tokens, false-abandon 0.000 every time.

\subsection{No novelty in the mechanism; the contribution is the grounding}\label{sec:giveup-mechanism}

The mechanism is old and we claim none of it: our gate is the trip half of a circuit breaker
(Nygard 2007), the degenerate corner of an SPRT (Wald 1945\cite{wald}), and a CUSUM (Page
1954\cite{page}) collapsed to a plain error tally. What is new is the grounding: (i) it counts only
environment-authored bytes the agent cannot forge; (ii) the threshold is calibrated against a
\emph{measured} false-abandon cost, not a guess; and (iii) it stays advisory --- it
\emph{proposes} a halt, never trips one --- so a mis-set \emph{K} degrades to a few wasted
tokens, never a killed winner.

\subsection{Every active fix failed}\label{sec:giveup-activefix}

Give-up is what was left standing after we tried the active fixes too --- the ones that author a
correction --- on the cross-benchmark target (EnterpriseOps-Gym, gemini-2.5-flash; these numbers
stay out of the Toolathlon tables and imply no Toolathlon lift).

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{WARN the agent} (re-surface the alarm into the loop): flat
on the natural failure stream, \textbf{+0.20pp}. The +6.2pp lift this
verb shows elsewhere held only when failures were artificially injected, not in
the wild.
  \item \textbf{Rewind} (content-free surgery --- cut the agent's bad
turns back out of the transcript): \textbf{0 conversions, 0 flips} on
20 paired fired tasks. The null only got stronger as we added data
(44 $\rightarrow$ 103 $\rightarrow$ 240 runs per arm).
  \item \textbf{The byte-clean cure} (re-surface the environment's own
schema correction --- the cleanest fix we could build): wired live, it
fired on 77 of 88 runs, and it was \textbf{net-negative}:
$-$5 task successes overall, $-$7 on the no-thrash slice (sign-test
p=0.016; successes fell 7 $\rightarrow$ 2 of 88). It converted the 2 runs it was aimed
at --- but injecting a corrective turn at all perturbed runs that would have
passed on their own.
\end{itemize}

\begin{quote}
\textbf{The harm is the intervention's existence in the loop, not the
content of its bytes.} Even a perfectly byte-clean correction perturbs
runs that were going to win. Only the negative action --- withhold compute
--- authors no turn. It perturbs nothing, and it is the only fix that
survives a measured bake-off.
\end{quote}

\subsection{Honest ceiling}\label{sec:giveup-ceiling}

This result proves \emph{specificity}, not \emph{sensitivity}: wherever the gate fires it never
halts a winner, but how often it fires we cannot promise. On Toolathlon at the sound threshold it
fires on not a single run (one fire at \emph{K}=2), because a capable model that fails
\emph{silently} emits no error to count --- the fires, and the saving, come from
EnterpriseOps-Gym (31 fires at \emph{K}=3, none on a winner). So the claim is
narrow: the gate is accuracy-free \emph{wherever it fires}, but the 11.4\% token saving is a
single-corpus, single-model figure. The specificity is the durable, regime-independent result; the
saving is real but travels less.
